reasoning: Confirmed real but downgraded to low severity. Code proof: class-bsr-main.php line 127 registers `admin_post_bsr_view_details` -> `BSR_Admin::load_details()`. WordPress admin_post_ requires login (blocks unauthenticated access) but does NOT require any capability. Inside load_details() (class-bsr-admin.php ~line 220), there is no current_user_can(), no check_admin_referer(), and no wp_verify_nonce() call. The adjacent handler download_sysinfo() explicitly calls BSR_Utils::check_admin_referer() (line 279) which checks both nonce and manage_options capability via bsr_enabled_for_user() — demonstrating the developer was aware of the pattern but omitted it from load_details(). The sink is confirmed reachable by any logged-in user (Subscriber+) via GET/POST to admin-post.php?action=bsr_view_details. However, the impact is low: load_details() only reads the bsr_results transient and renders a read-only table of the last search/replace run (table names + integer counts for changes/updates/time). No attacker-controlled input reaches any output sink — the transient was written by a privileged admin. No state mutation occurs. The exposure is information disclosure of database table names and row-change statistics to low-privilege users. This does not grant code execution, does not leak credentials or PII, and does not enable escalation. Severity is real but low, not medium.
